annotations.label: Projective space (via vector lines)

anchor.snippet: Using linear algebra , a projective space of dimension n is defined as the set of the vector lines

annotations.note: `Projectivization` is the projectivization of a vector space and `equivSubmodule` gives its bijection with the one-dimensional subspaces (vector lines), over any division ring.

annotations.label: Projective line and projective plane

anchor.snippet: A projective space of dimension 1 is a projective line , and a projective space of dimension 2 is a projective plane .

annotations.note: Projective lines/planes arise as special cases of `Projectivization` (e.g. `OnePoint.equivProjectivization` for the line), but are not separately named in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Projective space as set of vector lines

anchor.snippet: So, a projective space of dimension n can be defined as the set of vector lines

annotations.note: `equivSubmodule` realizes the projective space as the collection of one-dimensional subspaces (vector lines) of `V`.

annotations.label: Projective space via equivalence classes

anchor.snippet: This set can be the set of equivalence classes under the equivalence relation between vectors

annotations.note: `Projectivization` is literally defined as the quotient by `projectivizationSetoid`, the scalar-multiple equivalence on nonzero vectors.

annotations.label: Projective space as antipodal point pairs

anchor.snippet: A third equivalent definition is to define a projective space of dimension n as the set of pairs of antipodal points

annotations.note: Mathlib has no construction of (real) projective space as the sphere quotient by the antipodal map.

annotations.label: Projective space P(V)

anchor.snippet: Given a vector space V over a field K , the projective space P ( V ) is the set of equivalence classes

annotations.note: `Projectivization K V` (notation `ℙ K V`) is exactly P(V), defined for any division ring `K`, generalizing the field case.

annotations.label: Projective coordinates

anchor.snippet: the projective coordinates of a point P are the coordinates on the basis of any element of the corresponding equivalence class

annotations.note: `Projectivization.rep` chooses a representative vector whose basis coordinates would be the projective coordinates, but no homogeneous-coordinate API is defined.

annotations.label: Real and complex projective space

anchor.snippet: If K is the field of real or complex numbers, a projective space is called a real projective space or a complex projective space

annotations.note: These are the instances `ℙ ℝ V` and `ℙ ℂ V` of `Projectivization`, but Mathlib provides no dedicated real/complex projective space with topology or manifold structure.

annotations.label: Riemann sphere

anchor.snippet: The complex projective line is also called the Riemann sphere .

annotations.note: `OnePoint.equivProjectivization` identifies `OnePoint ℂ` (ℂ ∪ {∞}) with the complex projective line `ℙ ℂ (Fin 2 → ℂ)`, but the Riemann sphere as a complex manifold is not formalized.

annotations.label: Linear subspace is a union of lines

anchor.snippet: Every linear subspace W of V is a union of lines.

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma states that a submodule is the union of its one-dimensional subspaces.

annotations.label: Projective subspace

anchor.snippet: A projective subspace is thus a projective space that is obtained by restricting to a linear subspace

annotations.note: `Projectivization.Subspace` defines projective subspaces, with an order-isomorphism `Subspace.submodule` to the submodules of `V`.

annotations.label: Unique line through two points

anchor.snippet: There is exactly one projective line that passes through two different points of P ( V )

annotations.note: The span of a set of points is available via `Subspace.span`, but there is no lemma asserting a unique line through two distinct points.

annotations.label: Intersection of projective subspaces

anchor.snippet: Every intersection of projective subspaces is a projective subspace.

annotations.note: `Subspace` has an `InfSet`/`CompleteLattice` instance, so arbitrary intersections of projective subspaces are again subspaces.

annotations.label: Projective span and spanning set

anchor.snippet: This projective subspace is called the projective span of S , and S is a spanning set for it.

annotations.note: `Subspace.span` is the projective span of a set of points, given as a Galois insertion with the subset-of-points functor.

annotations.label: Projectively independent set

anchor.snippet: A set S of points is projectively independent if its span is not the span of any proper subset of S .

annotations.note: `Projectivization.Independent` formalizes independence of a family of projective points via linear independence of representatives, an equivalent formulation.

annotations.label: Existence of independent spanning subset

anchor.snippet: If S is a spanning set of a projective space P , then there is a subset of S that spans P and is projectively independent

annotations.note: No projective-space analogue of basis extraction from a spanning set is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Projective frame / basis

anchor.snippet: A projective frame or projective basis is an ordered set of points in a projective space that allows defining coordinates.

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define projective frames/bases (points in general position determining coordinates).

annotations.label: Frame characterization via basis

anchor.snippet: is a projective frame if and only if

annotations.note: No characterization of projective frames in terms of underlying vector-space bases is formalized.

annotations.label: Homogeneous coordinates on a frame

anchor.snippet: The projective coordinates or homogeneous coordinates of a point

annotations.note: Homogeneous coordinates relative to a projective frame are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Canonical frame

anchor.snippet: The canonical frame of the projective space

annotations.note: The canonical projective frame is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Projective geometry

anchor.snippet: projective geometry is the study of geometric properties that are invariant with respect to projective transformations

annotations.note: This describes a field of study rather than a single formalizable statement; Mathlib has no such overarching development.

annotations.label: Homography

anchor.snippet: a homography is an isomorphism of projective spaces

annotations.note: Homographies correspond to the `PGL` action on `ℙ`, which exists (`ProjGenLinGroup` and `Projectivization.map`), but homographies are not defined as a named isomorphism of projective spaces.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of projective geometry

anchor.snippet: the fundamental theorem of projective geometry asserts that is not so in the case of real projective spaces of dimension at least two

annotations.note: The fundamental theorem of projective geometry is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Projective space is a topological space

anchor.snippet: A projective space is a topological space , as endowed with the quotient topology

annotations.note: No quotient topology or `TopologicalSpace` instance is placed on `Projectivization`.

annotations.label: Projective space is a manifold

anchor.snippet: This shows that a projective space is a manifold.

annotations.note: No charted-space/manifold structure on projective space is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Projective spaces are analytic manifolds

anchor.snippet: it results that projective spaces are analytic manifolds

annotations.note: No analytic-manifold structure on projective space is formalized.

annotations.label: CW complex structure of real projective space

anchor.snippet: Real projective spaces have a simple CW complex structure

annotations.note: Mathlib has no CW-complex structure for real projective space.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of algebra

anchor.snippet: the fundamental theorem of algebra asserts that a univariate square-free polynomial of degree n has exactly n complex roots

annotations.note: `Complex.exists_root` (and `Complex.isAlgClosed`) is the fundamental theorem of algebra, from which the degree-`n` root count follows.

annotations.label: Bézout's theorem

anchor.snippet: Bézout's theorem asserts that the intersection of two plane algebraic curves of respective degrees d and e consists of exactly de points

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Bezout` file concerns Bézout rings/domains, not the intersection theorem for plane curves, which is unformalized.

annotations.label: Projective variety

anchor.snippet: So a projective variety is the set of points in a projective space, whose homogeneous coordinates are common zeros of a set of homogeneous polynomials

annotations.note: `ProjectiveSpectrum.zeroLocus` gives the (scheme-theoretic) common-zero loci of homogeneous elements, the analogue of projective varieties, but classical projective varieties are not separately developed.

annotations.label: Completion of an affine variety

anchor.snippet: Any affine variety can be completed , in a unique way, into a projective variety by adding its points at infinity

annotations.note: The projective closure of an affine variety is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Image of projective variety is closed

anchor.snippet: the image of a projective variety under a morphism of algebraic varieties is closed for Zariski topology

annotations.note: `Proj.toSpecZero` is proven `UniversallyClosed`/`IsProper` over `Spec 𝒜₀`, the scheme-theoretic completeness expressing that images of projective schemes are closed.

annotations.label: Projective space is a projective variety

anchor.snippet: A projective space is itself a projective variety, being the set of zeros of the zero polynomial.

annotations.note: The whole space is the zero locus of `0` via `zeroLocus`, but this triviality is not stated as a named lemma.

annotations.label: Proj construction

anchor.snippet: The Proj construction is the construction of the scheme of a projective space

annotations.note: `AlgebraicGeometry.Proj` constructs the scheme `Proj 𝒜` for any ℕ-graded ring `A`.

annotations.label: Axiomatic projective space

anchor.snippet: a projective space S can be defined axiomatically as a set P (the set of points), together with a set L of subsets of P

annotations.note: Mathlib has no axiomatic/synthetic incidence-geometry definition of projective space.

annotations.label: Veblen–Young theorem (dimension ≥ 3)

anchor.snippet: If the (projective) dimension is at least three then, by the Veblen–Young theorem , there is no difference.

annotations.note: The Veblen–Young coordinatization theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pappus's theorem (as axiom)

anchor.snippet: If the six vertices of a hexagon lie alternately on two lines, the three points of intersection of pairs of opposite sides are collinear.

annotations.note: Pappus's theorem in projective geometry is not formalized in Mathlib (only mentioned in docs).

annotations.label: Fano's axiom

anchor.snippet: The three diagonal points of a complete quadrangle are never collinear.

annotations.note: Fano's axiom on diagonal points of a complete quadrangle is not formalized.

annotations.label: Subspace (synthetic)

anchor.snippet: A subspace of the projective space is a subset X

annotations.note: `Projectivization.Subspace` captures the line-closure subspace condition, but only in the linear (not purely synthetic) setting.

annotations.label: Geometric dimension

anchor.snippet: The geometric dimension of the space is said to be n if that is the largest number

annotations.note: There is no notion of projective/geometric dimension via maximal chains of subspaces in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: The geometric dimension of the space is said to be n if that is the largest number

annotations.label: Correspondence with geomodular lattices

anchor.snippet: There is a bijective correspondence between projective spaces and geomodular lattices

annotations.note: The correspondence between projective spaces and geomodular lattices is not formalized.

annotations.label: Classification: dimension 0

anchor.snippet: Dimension 0 (no lines): The space is a single point.

annotations.note: `card_of_finrank` with `finrank = 1` yields a single point (`Nat.card (ℙ k V) = 1`), but the synthetic dim-0 classification is not stated.

annotations.label: Classification: dimension 1

anchor.snippet: Dimension 1 (exactly one line): All points lie on the unique line.

annotations.note: The synthetic classification of dimension-1 projective spaces is not formalized.

annotations.label: Classification: dimension 2 equals projective plane

anchor.snippet: A projective space for n = 2 is equivalent to a projective plane .

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize the synthetic equivalence of dimension-2 projective spaces with projective planes.

annotations.label: Classification: dimension ≥ 3 isomorphic to PG(n,K)

anchor.snippet: every projective space of dimension n ≥ 3 is isomorphic with a PG( n , K )

annotations.note: The coordinatization of dimension ≥ 3 projective spaces as PG(n,K) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Finite projective space

anchor.snippet: A finite projective space is a projective space where P is a finite set of points.

annotations.note: `finite_of_finite` shows `ℙ k V` is finite when `V` is finite, but 'finite projective space' is not a named concept.

annotations.label: Order of a finite projective space

anchor.snippet: the order of the space is defined as one less than this common number

annotations.note: The order of a finite projective space/plane is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wedderburn's theorem implication

anchor.snippet: Wedderburn's theorem implies that the division ring over which the projective space is defined must be a finite field

annotations.note: `littleWedderburn` is exactly Wedderburn's little theorem: every finite division ring is a (commutative) field.

annotations.label: Fano plane PG(2,2)

anchor.snippet: The smallest projective plane is the Fano plane , PG(2, 2) with 7 points and 7 lines.

annotations.note: `card_of_finrank` gives 7 points for `ℙ (ZMod 2) (Fin 3 → ZMod 2)`, but the Fano plane and its line incidences are not named/developed.

annotations.label: PG(3,2)

anchor.snippet: The smallest 3-dimensional projective space is PG(3, 2) , with 15 points, 35 lines and 15 planes.

annotations.note: `card_of_finrank` yields 15 points for `ℙ (ZMod 2) (Fin 4 → ZMod 2)`, but the line/plane counts of PG(3,2) are not formalized.

annotations.label: Injective maps induce projective maps

anchor.snippet: Injective linear maps T ∈ L ( V , W ) between two vector spaces V and W over the same field K induce mappings of the corresponding projective spaces

annotations.note: `Projectivization.map` builds the induced map on projective spaces from an injective semilinear map, generalizing the linear case.

annotations.label: Maps agree iff scalar multiple

anchor.snippet: Two linear maps S and T in L ( V , W ) induce the same map between P ( V ) and P ( W ) if and only if they differ by a scalar multiple

annotations.note: No lemma states that two linear maps induce the same projective map iff they are scalar multiples.

annotations.label: Algebraic automorphisms are linear

anchor.snippet: any algebraic (not necessarily linear) automorphism must be linear

annotations.note: The result that algebraic automorphisms of projective space are induced by (semi)linear maps is not formalized.

annotations.label: Projective linear groups PGL

anchor.snippet: The groups PGL are called projective linear groups .

annotations.note: `Matrix.ProjGenLinGroup` (notation `PGL(n, R)`) is the projective general linear group, the quotient of `GL n R` by its center.

annotations.label: Möbius transformations

anchor.snippet: The automorphisms of the complex projective line P 1 ( C ) are called Möbius transformations .

annotations.note: The `GL(2,K)` action on `OnePoint K`/`ℙ¹` is given explicitly by Möbius transformations, but no named 'Möbius transformation' group of P¹(ℂ) is defined.

annotations.label: Dual projective space

anchor.snippet: one obtains the dual projective space, which can be canonically identified with the space of hyperplanes through the origin of V

annotations.note: The dual projective space (projectivization of the dual / space of hyperplanes) is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Grassmannian manifold

anchor.snippet: is generalized to Grassmannian manifold , which is parametrizing higher-dimensional subspaces

annotations.note: `Module.Grassmannian` defines Grassmannians (via locally free quotients) over a ring, generalizing the subspace Grassmannian, though not as a smooth manifold.

annotations.label: Flag manifold

anchor.snippet: flag manifold is the space of flags, i.e., chains of linear subspaces of V

annotations.note: Mathlib has order-theoretic `Flag` (maximal chains) but no flag variety/manifold of chains of subspaces.

annotations.label: Severi–Brauer varieties

anchor.snippet: Severi–Brauer varieties are algebraic varieties over a field K , which become isomorphic to projective spaces after an extension of the base field K

annotations.note: Severi–Brauer varieties are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Weighted projective spaces

anchor.snippet: Another generalization of projective spaces are weighted projective spaces

annotations.note: Weighted projective spaces are not formalized in Mathlib.
